title: Fixing HTTP 404 Errors in Spring MVC

message.text:
HTTP Status 404 – Not Found
Type Status Report

Description The origin server did not find a current representation for the target resource or is not willing to disclose that one exists.

Apache Tomcat/10.1.54

editAgentRounds.reply: I'll search the project for your WebMvcConfig and other Spring MVC config/classes and web.xml or servlet initializer to understand why requests return 404. I'll look for files named WebMvcConfig, controllers, and mappings.

toolCalls.name: grep_search
toolCalls.progressMessage: Searched for text in files matching query: WebMvcConfig

toolCalls.name: grep_search
toolCalls.progressMessage: Searched for text in files matching query: @Controller

editAgentRounds.reply: I'll update `AdminController.java` to return text responses (add @ResponseBody and return String) so DispatcherServlet produces a response instead of resolving a non-existent view which causes 404. I'll apply the change and then run a project error check.

Task received — I'll diagnose the 404 and make a small, safe fix so your controller actually returns a response instead of letting Spring try to resolve a missing view (which is the likely cause of the 404). Plan:
- Inspect the config and controller files.
- Explain the cause of the 404.
- Make a minimal code change so the controller returns a response body for quick testing.
- Give steps to redeploy and next options (view resolver / JSP) if you want view pages.

Checklist
- [x] Locate DispatcherServlet initializer and controller code
- [x] Identify likely cause of 404
- [x] Update controller to return text responses (@ResponseBody)
- [x] Tell you how to redeploy and test
- [ ] (Optional) Add view resolver or JSPs if you prefer HTML pages (I can add these next)

What I found
- Your dispatcher servlet initializer class `springMVC1.config.WEbAppInitialize` is present and mapped to "/" which is correct.
- `springMVC1.config.WebMvcConfig` has @EnableWebMvc and @ComponentScan("springMVC1.controller"), so controllers are scanned.
- `springMVC1.controller.AdminController` had handler methods that return void. When a controller method returns void and doesn't write to the response, Spring will attempt to resolve a view based on the request. If the view (JSP, etc.) is missing, you will get a 404. This is the most likely reason you saw HTTP Status 404.
  - The compiled classes are present in target/, so the app is being deployed, but the handlers produced no response/view.

What I changed
- Edited `AdminController.java` to return simple text with @ResponseBody so calls to /insert and /delete return a visible response instead of trying to resolve a non-existent view.

Files changed
- c:\Users\Mallikarjun N\Desktop\eclipse_data\springMVC1\src\main\java\springMVC1\controller\AdminController.java

Key code change (summary)
- Added import:
  - org.springframework.web.bind.annotation.ResponseBody
- Changed methods:
  - insertAdmin() now: @RequestMapping("/insert") @ResponseBody public String insertAdmin() { ... return "inserted"; }
  - deleteAdmin() now: @RequestMapping("/delete") @ResponseBody public String deleteAdmin() { ... return "deleted"; }

How to test (redeploy & check)
- If you run Tomcat from Eclipse:
  - Clean & republish the project (right-click server → Clean or Republish), or stop and start the server.
- Hit the endpoints in your browser or curl:
  - Browser:
    - http://localhost:8080/<context>/insert
    - http://localhost:8080/<context>/delete
    - Replace <context> with your webapp context path (often the project name, e.g. springMVC1).
  - curl (from cmd.exe):
    ```
    curl http://localhost:8080/springMVC1/insert
    curl http://localhost:8080/springMVC1/delete
    ```
  - Expected responses: the body will contain "inserted" and "deleted" respectively and you should see the console prints from the methods.

Why this fixes the 404
- Returning @ResponseBody ensures Spring writes a response, so no view resolution is attempted. Previously Spring attempted to find a view for a view name derived from the request (e.g. "insert"), and since no JSP/view resolver or JSP file existed, you got a 404.

Next suggestions (optional)
- If you want the controller to render JSP pages, do one of the following:
  1. Configure an InternalResourceViewResolver bean in `WebMvcConfig`:
     - Example:
       ```
       @Bean
       public InternalResourceViewResolver viewResolver() {
           InternalResourceViewResolver r = new InternalResourceViewResolver();
           r.setPrefix("/WEB-INF/views/");
           r.setSuffix(".jsp");
           return r;
       }
       ```
     - Then return view names from your controller methods (e.g., return "insert";) and add the JSP `/WEB-INF/views/insert.jsp`.
  2. Keep the @ResponseBody approach for REST-like endpoints, or return ResponseEntity for more control.
- You can also change method return types to `String` (view name) and add JSP views if you prefer server-side rendered pages.

If you'd like I can:
- Add an InternalResourceViewResolver to `WebMvcConfig` and create a simple JSP under `src/main/webapp/WEB-INF/views/insert.jsp` and `delete.jsp` so the original void/String behavior works.
- Or convert controllers to @RestController which implies @ResponseBody for all methods.
- Remove the (unused) org.jspecify.annotations.Nullable in the initializer if you want cleaner code (not required).

Would you like me to:
- Add view resolver + example JSPs, or
- Convert all controllers to REST style (e.g., @RestController), or
- Make any other changes and re-run compilation checks?

Tell me which option and I will implement it.

turns.suggestedTitle: Fixing HTTP 404 Errors in Spring MVC
